branchingScenario.title: PBL: Histology & Embryology (Abdomen & Pelvis) — Clinical Reasoning

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Work through this clinical case to test your understanding of key concepts in GIT histology and abdominal embryology. Make diagnostic and management decisions based on the clinical scenario.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You made the correct clinical decisions at each step. You demonstrated strong understanding of the underlying concepts. Well done!
You showed some understanding but chose a suboptimal path. Review the feedback provided and try again to reinforce your learning.
Your clinical reasoning needs strengthening in this area. Review the source material carefully, paying attention to the key concepts highlighted, then attempt the case again.

params.text:
Case: Neonatal Vomiting

A 2-day-old neonate presents with bilious vomiting since birth. The baby has not passed meconium. Abdominal X-ray shows a "double-bubble" sign. The baby also has features suggestive of Down syndrome.

Key concept: The double-bubble sign suggests duodenal atresia, caused by failure of recanalisation of the duodenum during development. The epithelium of the GIT is derived from endoderm.

branchingQuestion.question: The X-ray shows a double-bubble sign. What embryological mechanism causes duodenal atresia?

alternatives.text: Failure of recanalisation of the duodenum during development

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The duodenum undergoes solid-stage occlusion and then recanalises. Failure of this process causes duodenal atresia with the classic double-bubble sign.

alternatives.text: Failure of migration of neural crest cells to the duodenum

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Failure of neural crest cell migration causes Hirschsprung disease (aganglionosis of distal bowel), not duodenal atresia.

alternatives.text: Abnormal rotation of the midgut around the superior mesenteric artery

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Abnormal midgut rotation causes malrotation and volvulus, not duodenal atresia. Duodenal atresia is due to failure of recanalisation.

params.text:
Surgical Findings

At surgery, complete duodenal atresia is confirmed. A duodenoduodenostomy is performed. Biopsy of the duodenal wall is taken for histology. The pathologist identifies Brunner's glands in the submucosa, confirming duodenal tissue.

Concept: The GIT wall has a universal four-layer structure: mucosa, submucosa, muscularis externa, and serosa/adventitia. Brunner's glands in the submucosa are the distinguishing feature of the duodenum, while Peyer's patches identify the ileum.

branchingQuestion.question: How did the pathologist confirm this was duodenal tissue? What is the distinguishing histological feature of the duodenum?

alternatives.text: Brunner's glands in the submucosa — unique to the duodenum

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Brunner's glands (mucous-secreting glands in the submucosa) are pathognomonic for the duodenum. Peyer's patches identify the ileum.

alternatives.text: Peyer's patches in the submucosa identify all parts of the small intestine

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Peyer's patches (aggregated lymphoid follicles) are the distinguishing feature of the ileum, not the duodenum.

alternatives.text: Parietal cells in the mucosa confirm duodenal tissue

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Parietal (oxyntic) cells are found in the fundic glands of the stomach, not the duodenum. They secrete HCl and intrinsic factor.

params.text:
Clinical Resolution

The baby recovers well after surgery. Genetic testing confirms Trisomy 21. The parents are counselled about associated conditions.

Learning points:

  • GIT wall: mucosa, submucosa, muscularis externa, serosa/adventitia
  • Brunner's glands (submucosa) = duodenum; Peyer's patches = ileum
  • GIT epithelium derives from endoderm; liver and pancreas bud from foregut
  • Hirschsprung disease = failure of neural crest cell migration
  • Kidney develops from intermediate mesoderm
